concepts.title: Garbage in, gospel out — the data-quality dimensions

beats.text: A model trained on broken data does not announce it; it produces a confident number on a clean dashboard.

visual.sub: Auditing the inputs before the model

beats.text: The first skill is not modelling but auditing inputs along seven concrete data-quality dimensions.
Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, uniqueness, validity, and representativeness each have a question they ask.
Each dimension bites a model in a different way, such as a sentinel age of minus three masquerading as real data.

visual.label: Sentinel value posing as an age

beats.text: Labels are data too, and benchmark audits found error rates of a few percent in canonical test sets like ImageNet.

visual.label: Label-error rate in ImageNet test set

beats.text: Read your data by hand before you trust any number computed from it.
Machine learning promises a model will generalize to data it has never seen, which is only credible if some data stays unseen.
The dataset splits into three disjoint roles where no row, and no information about a row, appears in more than one part.

visual.note: EQ D1.1 — the disjoint three-way split

beats.text: A common allocation is seventy, fifteen, fifteen, so a thousand-row dataset puts one hundred fifty rows in the test set.
The test set is touched once at the very end; the moment you tune against it, it becomes a second validation set.

visual.label: Times the test set may be touched

beats.text: The standard error of a test metric falls like one over the square root of the test-set size.

visual.note: EQ D1.2 — standard error of a proportion

beats.text: To halve your uncertainty you must quadruple the test set, so the band shrinks as test rows grow.

concepts.title: Data leakage — the most expensive bug in ML

beats.text: Leakage is any path by which information that will not exist at prediction time reaches the model and inflates its score.

visual.sub: The most expensive bug in ML

beats.text: Three families cover almost every case: target leakage, temporal leakage, and group leakage.
A feature is admissible only if it is knowable at prediction time, before the label is realized.

visual.tex: x_j \text{ admissible} \iff x_j \text{ knowable at } t_{\text{pred}}, \text{ before } y

beats.text: Fitting a scaler on the full data before splitting lets training rows see the mean and variance of the test rows.

visual.label: Test rows the scaler should ever see

beats.text: Group leakage scatters one entity across train and test, so the model meets a test patient it has already seen.
Turn on a leaked feature and validation accuracy climbs toward one while true holdout barely moves; the gap is the lie.
A split keeps test data unseen but cannot guarantee it resembles the world the model will actually face.

visual.sub: When the data stops matching the world

beats.text: Evaluation is valid only when train, test, and deployment distributions are approximately the same.
Three shifts break it: covariate shift moves the input mix, label shift moves the base rate, concept drift moves the rule.
When time matters, a random split lets the model train on the future and test on the past, so use a time-based split.
Sampling bias sits upstream; if the data was never collected, no split or model can recover it.

visual.label: Population a clever estimator can conjure

concepts.title: Building the modeling dataset — a protocol

beats.text: Here is the order of operations that keeps every later number honest, because most leakage is an ordering bug.
The protocol runs in eight steps, from defining the target to evaluating with error bars.
Define the target, audit features against the leakage criterion, dedup, then split before anything else touches the data.
Fit scalers and encoders on training data only, then apply those training statistics to validation and test.

visual.label: Training rows the scaler is fit on

beats.text: Decontaminate by hashing rows to confirm no training example reappears in validation or test.
Tune on validation, touch test once, and report with the standard-error bars from the test contract.

visual.note: report results with EQ D1.2 error bars
